name: Sphingolipid metabolism
summary: The pathway that builds ceramide and complex sphingolipids from L-serine and palmitoyl-CoA and turns them over into the bioactive signals ceramide, sphingosine, and sphingosine 1-phosphate; serine palmitoyltransferase is the committed, ORMDL-regulated step.
description: Sphingolipids are built on a sphingoid-base backbone rather than glycerol. De novo synthesis begins on the cytosolic face of the endoplasmic reticulum, where the pyridoxal-5'-phosphate–dependent serine palmitoyltransferase (SPT, SPTLC1/SPTLC2) condenses L-serine with palmitoyl-CoA and decarboxylates it to 3-ketodihydrosphingosine — the committed and rate-limiting step. KDSR reduces this to sphinganine, a ceramide synthase (CERS1–6) N-acylates it with a fatty acyl-CoA to dihydroceramide, and dihydroceramide desaturase (DES1/DEGS1) introduces the 4,5-trans double bond to give ceramide, the metabolic hub of the pathway. Ceramide moves to the Golgi where sphingomyelin synthase transfers phosphocholine from phosphatidylcholine (releasing diacylglycerol) to make sphingomyelin, and glucosylceramide synthase adds glucose from UDP-glucose to launch the glycosphingolipids. Turnover runs the reactions in reverse compartments: sphingomyelinases regenerate ceramide from sphingomyelin (the stress-activated 'sphingomyelin cycle'), and ceramidases hydrolyze ceramide to sphingosine. Sphingosine is either salvaged back to ceramide by ceramide synthases (the quantitatively dominant salvage pathway) or phosphorylated by sphingosine kinase to sphingosine 1-phosphate (S1P). S1P is dephosphorylated back to sphingosine by S1P phosphatase, or irreversibly cleaved by the PLP-dependent S1P lyase to ethanolamine phosphate and (2E)-hexadecenal — the single exit that permanently removes carbon from the sphingolipid pool and feeds it into glycerophospholipid and fatty-acid metabolism. The opposing pro-apoptotic/growth-arrest actions of ceramide and the pro-survival, pro-migratory actions of S1P form the 'sphingolipid rheostat'. Disease links are direct: SPTLC1 mutations cause hereditary sensory neuropathy, acid sphingomyelinase (SMPD1) deficiency causes Niemann–Pick disease A/B, acid ceramidase (ASAH1) deficiency causes Farber lipogranulomatosis, and glucosylceramide accumulation underlies Gaucher disease.

sources.id: Hannun & Obeid 2018, Nat Rev Mol Cell Biol 19:175-191
Lehninger Principles of Biochemistry, 8e, Ch. 10 & 21 (Lipids; Biosynthesis of Membrane Lipids)

references.title: Sphingolipids and their metabolism in physiology and disease.
Orm family proteins mediate sphingolipid homeostasis.
Activation of sphingosine kinase 1 by ERK1/2-mediated phosphorylation.
The sphingolipid salvage pathway in ceramide metabolism and signaling.

compartments.name: Endoplasmic reticulum
compartments.description: Cytosolic face of the ER hosts the entire de novo sequence (SPT, KDSR, ceramide synthase, DES1) and the S1P phosphatase and S1P lyase that terminate the pathway.

compartments.name: Golgi apparatus
compartments.description: Where ceramide receives its polar head groups: sphingomyelin synthase (trans-Golgi) and glucosylceramide synthase (cytosolic face of cis-Golgi).

compartments.name: Lysosome
compartments.description: Acid sphingomyelinase and acid ceramidase catabolize sphingomyelin and ceramide here, feeding the salvage pathway.

compartments.name: Cytosol
compartments.description: Sphingosine kinase 1 and the signaling kinase ERK2 act in the cytosol; activated SphK1 translocates to the plasma membrane.

metabolites.name: Fatty acyl-CoA
metabolites.description: Generic long-chain fatty acyl-CoA donor used by ceramide synthases; chain length (C14–C26) sets the ceramide species and is CERS-isoform specific.

metabolites.name: Ferrocytochrome b5
metabolites.description: Reduced cytochrome b5; supplies the two electrons for the DES1 desaturation, re-reduced by cytochrome b5 reductase at the expense of NAD(P)H.

metabolites.name: Ceramide
metabolites.description: Central hub of sphingolipid metabolism and a pro-apoptotic/growth-arrest second messenger; the counterweight to S1P in the sphingolipid rheostat.

metabolites.name: Phosphatidylcholine
metabolites.description: Phosphocholine donor for sphingomyelin synthase; consumed 1:1 with ceramide, coupling the sphingolipid and glycerophospholipid pools.

metabolites.name: Sphingomyelin
metabolites.description: Major membrane sphingolipid, enriched with cholesterol in lipid rafts and in the myelin sheath.

metabolites.name: 1,2-Diacyl-sn-glycerol
metabolites.description: Released by sphingomyelin synthase; a PKC-activating second messenger and a link to glycerolipid synthesis.

metabolites.name: Glucosylceramide
metabolites.description: Simplest glycosphingolipid and the common precursor of lactosylceramide, gangliosides and globosides.

metabolites.name: Fatty acid
metabolites.description: Generic long-chain fatty acid released by ceramidase hydrolysis of ceramide.

metabolites.name: Sphingosine
metabolites.description: Free sphingoid base produced by ceramidase; either salvaged to ceramide or phosphorylated to S1P.

metabolites.name: Sphingosine 1-phosphate
metabolites.description: Pro-survival, pro-migratory, pro-angiogenic signaling lipid; ligand for the S1P1–5 GPCRs and the counterweight to ceramide in the sphingolipid rheostat.

metabolites.name: Ethanolamine phosphate
metabolites.description: Product of S1P lyase; feeds the CDP-ethanolamine (Kennedy) pathway for phosphatidylethanolamine synthesis.

metabolites.name: (2E)-Hexadecenal
metabolites.description: Product of S1P lyase; oxidized to (2E)-hexadecenoate and activated to palmitoyl-CoA, recycling the sphingoid carbon skeleton into fatty-acid/glycerolipid metabolism.

metabolites.name: Pyridoxal 5'-phosphate
metabolites.description: Prosthetic group of the two amino-acid-handling enzymes of the pathway: serine palmitoyltransferase and S1P lyase.

metabolites.name: Tumor necrosis factor alpha (TNF-alpha)
metabolites.description: Inflammatory cytokine / death-receptor ligand that activates sphingomyelinases (the sphingomyelin cycle) to generate ceramide as a stress second messenger.

enzymes.name: Serine palmitoyltransferase long chain base subunit 1
enzymes.description: Non-catalytic membrane subunit that partners the PLP-binding SPTLC2 in the SPT complex.

enzymes.name: Serine palmitoyltransferase long chain base subunit 2
enzymes.description: PLP-dependent catalytic subunit of SPT; carries the active-site lysine that forms the Schiff base with serine.

enzymes.name: Ceramide synthase 2
enzymes.description: One of six human ceramide synthases (CERS1–6) with distinct acyl-CoA chain-length preferences; CERS2 makes very-long-chain (C22–C24) ceramides. The same enzymes acylate sphinganine (de novo) and sphingosine (salvage).

enzymes.name: Sphingolipid delta(4)-desaturase DES1
enzymes.description: Introduces the 4,5-trans double bond that converts dihydroceramide to ceramide, using O2 and electrons delivered from NAD(P)H via cytochrome b5.

enzymes.name: Sphingomyelin synthase 1
enzymes.family: sphingomyelin synthase / lipid phosphate phosphatase superfamily
enzymes.description: Trans-Golgi phosphatidylcholine:ceramide cholinephosphotransferase; transfers phosphocholine from PC to ceramide, releasing diacylglycerol.

enzymes.name: Ceramide glucosyltransferase (glucosylceramide synthase)
enzymes.description: Cytosolic-facing cis-Golgi enzyme that adds glucose from UDP-glucose to ceramide, the committed step of glycosphingolipid synthesis.

enzymes.name: Acid sphingomyelinase
enzymes.description: Lysosomal/secreted sphingomyelinase; loss-of-function causes Niemann–Pick disease types A and B.

enzymes.name: Acid ceramidase
enzymes.description: Lysosomal ceramidase that hydrolyzes ceramide to sphingosine and a fatty acid; deficiency causes Farber lipogranulomatosis.

enzymes.name: Sphingosine kinase 1
enzymes.description: Phosphorylates sphingosine to S1P; activated by ERK1/2 phosphorylation at Ser225 and translocation to the plasma membrane.

enzymes.name: Sphingosine-1-phosphate phosphatase 1
enzymes.description: ER enzyme that dephosphorylates S1P back to sphingosine, opposing sphingosine kinase and favoring re-entry into ceramide.

enzymes.name: Sphingosine-1-phosphate lyase 1
enzymes.description: PLP-dependent lyase that irreversibly cleaves S1P to ethanolamine phosphate and (2E)-hexadecenal — the terminal, committed exit of sphingolipid metabolism.

enzymes.name: ORM1-like protein 3 (ORMDL3)
enzymes.family: ORMDL / ORM family ER membrane protein
enzymes.description: Small ER transmembrane protein that binds the SPT complex and inhibits it in a ceramide-dependent manner, mediating homeostatic feedback; the ORMDL3 locus is a childhood-asthma risk factor.

enzymes.name: Mitogen-activated protein kinase 1 (ERK2)
enzymes.description: Agonist-activated ERK1/2 phosphorylates SphK1 at Ser225 to boost S1P production.

complexes.name: Serine palmitoyltransferase (SPT) complex
complexes.description: ER-membrane heterodimer of SPTLC1 and the PLP-dependent catalytic SPTLC2, assembled with a small activating subunit (ssSPTa/b; SPTSSA/SPTSSB) and negatively regulated by ORMDL proteins. Catalyzes the committed, rate-limiting step of sphingolipid synthesis.

cofactors.role: PLP prosthetic group (Schiff base with serine)

reactions.name: Dihydroceramide desaturation (DES1)
reactions.equation: Dihydroceramide + 2 Ferrocytochrome b5 + O2 + 2 H+ -> Ceramide + 2 Ferricytochrome b5 + 2 H2O

reactions.name: Glucosylceramide synthase (committed step of glycosphingolipid synthesis)
Salvage re-acylation of sphingosine to ceramide

provenance.notes: Salvage pathway: free sphingosine liberated by ceramide/sphingolipid turnover is re-acylated to ceramide by the same ceramide synthases (CERS1-6) that act on sphinganine in de novo synthesis (KEGG R06517). This sphingosine-substrate reaction has no dedicated KEGG reaction record; it is grounded in the salvage-pathway literature (PMID 18191382) and is quantitatively the dominant route of ceramide production in many cell types.

regulations.mechanism: Homeostatic feedback: elevated ceramide (or a downstream sphingolipid) is sensed by ER ORMDL proteins bound to the SPT complex, which inhibit serine palmitoyltransferase — the committed step — capping de novo sphingolipid synthesis.

sources.id: Hannun & Obeid 2018, Nat Rev Mol Cell Biol 19:175-191

regulations.mechanism: ORMDL3, a small ER transmembrane protein, forms a stable complex with SPT (SPTLC1/SPTLC2) and inhibits catalysis in a ceramide-dependent manner, transducing the homeostatic feedback signal.

Agonist-activated ERK1/2 phosphorylates SphK1 at Ser225, raising catalytic efficiency ~14-fold and driving translocation to the plasma membrane, which increases S1P output (pro-survival/pro-migratory signaling).

Stress stimuli and death-receptor ligands (TNF-alpha, Fas/CD95) activate neutral and acid sphingomyelinases, hydrolyzing sphingomyelin to ceramide — the 'sphingomyelin cycle' that generates ceramide as a pro-apoptotic stress second messenger.

sources.id: Hannun & Obeid 2018, Nat Rev Mol Cell Biol 19:175-191

relations.note: SPTLC1 is the non-catalytic long-chain-base subunit of the SPT complex.
SPTLC2 is the PLP-dependent catalytic subunit of the SPT complex.
L-serine supplied by serine/glycine metabolism is condensed with palmitoyl-CoA at the committed SPT step, coupling serine availability to sphingolipid synthesis.
Palmitoyl-CoA from de novo lipogenesis / fatty-acid activation is the acyl donor for SPT, and long-chain acyl-CoAs feed the ceramide synthases, linking lipogenesis to sphingolipid supply.

Sphingomyelin synthase moves phosphocholine from phosphatidylcholine to ceramide, releasing diacylglycerol — coupling the sphingolipid and glycerophospholipid pools and generating a PKC-activating second messenger.
Ethanolamine phosphate from S1P lyase feeds the CDP-ethanolamine (Kennedy) pathway for phosphatidylethanolamine synthesis — the terminal metabolic exit of sphingolipids.
The (2E)-hexadecenal released by S1P lyase is oxidized to (2E)-hexadecenoate and activated to palmitoyl-CoA, recycling the sphingoid carbon skeleton into fatty-acid/glycerolipid metabolism.

S1P is exported (e.g., by SPNS2/MFSD2B) and acts on the S1P1-5 GPCRs ('inside-out' signaling) to control lymphocyte egress, vascular integrity, and tumor growth; intracellular S1P also has direct targets (HDACs, TRAF2).
Glucosylceramide is the common precursor for lactosylceramide and the complex glycosphingolipids (gangliosides, globosides).
